title: 2025 07 31 EDSI (1)
displayTitle: 2025-07-31 - Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee - Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of Crown Regional Holdings Limited
committee: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee
topic: Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of Crown Regional Holdings Limited
description: Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of Crown Regional Holdings Limited Crown Regional Holdings Ltd John Rae, Chair, Crown Regional Holdings Limited Robert Pigou, Deputy Secretary, Kānoa Regional Development & Commercial Services, Ministry of Business Innovation & Employment
descriptionSummary: Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of Crown Regional Holdings Limited. Crown Regional Holdings Ltd. John Rae, Chair, Crown Regional Holdings Limited

chunks.text: Deputy Chief Executive Regional Development and Commercial Services, and Joanne Taylor, who's our CFO. Um, I only tend to make a few introductory comments, um, on our 2024 annual report, and then we'll be happy to take any questions. So we're a schedule for company, um, and we simply hold the assets that are, uh, that areed to us by, uh, the government once they've provided funding to, uh, various companies.

Um, 2324, contrary to what you may have heard in the press, from our perspective, has been a pretty good year, particularly given the difficult circumstances that we have in the economy. Uh, and we are pretty positive about the role that we are playing in the regional economic growth story as at the end of June, we have 200 active con contracts totalling one just over a billion dollars.

Um, and as a result of the interest earned and repayments received in 2024, we paid a dividend to the crown of 19.2 million, completed a share buyback from shareholding ministers of 58 million and reduced the level of unsubscribed share capital of 40 million. So these actions improved the government's cash position by $117.3 million.

Um, the, the achievements set out in the annual report really focused on our drive to improve the, um, improve the outcomes of regional New Zealand and a series of projects that we are enabling through the regional infrastructure fund. Um, we have done, and you would've heard a lot of, um, investment in the Eastern Bay of Plenty, and that's the Portee Harbor Development.

It's one asset that is, uh, is the largest in the Crown Regional Holdings portfolio. Um, and that, uh, is seeing some significant improvements in regional employment, which we're very proud of. Uh, hopefully later on this year, we'll see the, um, the mussel farms, which activate the harbor and the, uh, mussels program, uh, improve the, the lot of, uh, broader cast of players in that region.

As I said earlier, we continue to operate in a pretty difficult macroeconomic environment. Um, and, uh, that has obviously an impact on our loan book, but we, we operate in a space where, uh, we only play where commercial lenders shouldn't, uh, don't play, and we shouldn't play in, in any other space.

If the market will solve some of these problems, then we shouldn't be playing. Um, and as an example, we were stepped into the North Island weather events, and we responded by providing significant funding to keep a number of businesses alive then. Uh, so that's as far all that I was really pleased to say. Um, I'd encourage, I'd like to acknowledge that my fellow board members, it's a interesting and, um, challenging space to do this, to operate in regional economic development.

Um, and, uh, thank you to Robert and, and Joanne for providing a very sound support to our ability to try and manage this portfolio as best we can. Thank you. I look forward to questions. Thanks, John. Um, members questions? Um, Scott will, Yes. Thank you. Um, so one of the things that, um, we're thinking about is how, how you are managing to, in, uh, accurately value or ensure that the new investments are, are valued accurately, because there's, as you say, it's, it's a very, um, impetuous environment.

Can, can I have a comment that Bit? Um, sure. Um, thank you very much for your question. Um, we, so we work with the major accounting firms to validate the basis upon which we value these investments. So, um, every year there is a process that we go through on the loan book and the equity investments to assess whether there needs to be any, uh, write down or write up or value based upon the broad range of factors that would be infected.

Those investments, not just, not just their cash positions and their trading positions, but the broader economy, the sector that they're trading in competition, whole range of things. And we have, uh, uh, one of the 4 1, 1 of the big quarters, the loan book and the other, um, another one does the, uh, equity investments.

And they've got sufficient information from the work that they do in the financial sector to be able to look at those and benchmark them against other businesses, other banks, for example, uh, to assess whether in fact we are valuing them correctly and taking an appropriate view on those values. Supplementary, That's actually a, not a primary.

Oh, the primary, yeah. Um, maybe help, help me out here. Mm-hmm. Um, crown Regional Holdings is, is essentially not choosing the winners, but is, is managing the contracts. So if we see, so you're sort of, you're, you're really responsive to where the direction is, is heading through tunnel.

Is that, is that correct? Yes. So, but is there any way for you to pass back, as you say, your, your, you are arriving in places where the market doesn't, doesn't wanna work. Is there any way for you to be back to say, we think there's, there's market failure in this region?

'cause we are certainly seeing market failure Yes. In the regions. That's a really good question. Um, and, and look, it is, we, we provide advice that accompanies the advice that goes to ministers from Kano, from on projects. Um, and we, at our board meetings, we provide our assessment of what we're seeing in the regions that the board is widely dispersed and has, uh, regional economic development footprint around the country.

And we're all really vitally interested in regional economic development, otherwise we wouldn't take this challenge on. So we do provide that feedback. Um, uh, and, but ultimately we don't get to choose, even if our advice is we should do stuff that often doesn't happen because ultimately it's, uh, it's the, the, the decisions are made by ministers and we can simply provide our advice.

We, we provide it freely and frankly, um, sometimes, uh, not in support of projects that are put forward and some that, some that are, um, uh, but, but we have got an interest in mapping where we think interventions would be most helpful. Um, examples of that are, um, and, and Robert and his team do a great job with being the, the wood processing sector, which is really under stress at the moment Mm.

Um, at a system level. Mm. And, um, we work hard with Kano to try and figure out what sort of interventions might be required to prevent wholesale dislocation of human capital around the country, um, which is a logical outcome if we don't find a solution. Mm-hmm. Does that help? Is it, uh, supplementary?

Yes. A supplementary. Supplementary, thank you. And so in terms of economic growth, what are the, what are the things that you consider do, do you have a narrow definition of economic growth, such as financial return, or do you, do you consider the wider impact such as job creation, um, you know, um, healthy, healthy, um, communities, um, the, the wider interpretation of economics that absolutely provide for wellbeing?

Yes. Could you give an example, some Examples? Uh, yeah. Okay. Thank you. Uh, so generally speaking, the, the government's funding is to provide public benefit rather than just commercial benefit. If that was the only reason, if commercial benefits were the only reasons we were making those investments, we would quickly run up against what the priority equity firms and the commercial banks were doing.

So the schemes and there've been 12 over the last eight years, have all, even though the criteria have changed, fundamentally they've been for a public good public benefit, which means creating job opportunities, creating training opportunities, improving the general economic plight and, and prospects for people in regions, um, enabling emerging technology to, to move forward, providing catalysts for, for businesses or, or new types of businesses that, that are not able to raise that last bit, or in some case, the first bit of, of capital

to actually generate these businesses to move forward, to prove that this is an industry that we could, that we can, uh, that we can help develop. Uh, and a is an example of that. I mean, it's terrible stats across every socioeconomic dimension you can think of. And this, the, the two big projects there, and maybe there's a third one that we'll develop over time.

Were primarily aimed at those objectives to try something different, to move the diet for those communities. Mm-hmm. Right. A supplementary from a Kush. Thank you Madam Chair. Continuing on, um, the reflections and insights you've just shared and using all portier as a really good, um, example of the regional growth, the economic growth, the, the positive social impacts.

What are the three key enablers you've learned that could help transfer that sort of success and enable that growth in other such communities? If you could narrow it down to three, feel free to share more. Well, I'll start, I'll give John time to think.

No, I heard that. That's wonder. I think that, um, the first lesson for me would be, um, starting a new business, starting a new industry in a community is not easy, right? There are, there are real challenges, uh, particularly to get people engaged and becoming part of that workforce who have four generations not had the opportunities to work.

And you have to teach people how to work, right? It's, it's, we saw it happen in Northland under the, some of the PGF programs. You almost need to create a mentor, sponsor, uh, arrangement with local people to help 'em understand what it means to actually be part of the workforce, to be part of a team, to actually develop, um, that combined effort to achieve the, the, the corporate and the social goals.

So that would be one of the first things that I think we've learned. The, the, the second one, um, for me is with the three year regional local government cycle, be very careful when you sign up to the aspiration. Remember, these were projects that were championed by local government, by and large, they, they came to us asking for support to do these projects when mayors and, um, and elected officials change quite often the political games that get played and not necessarily provide continuity for a project that is long

and duration, certainly longer than the three year election cycle. So we have to be careful at a system level to make sure that we protect projects against change of governments, which again, which are less about the regional, uh, aspiration than about politicking. So that's been, that's been a really insightful position.

And that would possibly also apply our investments in, in te Maori. And, and, uh, for fau, in that example, as the, the commitments that were made to support fau, we have to be careful to make sure that their aspirations at, at a timeline level, meet those of the fund to get things happening.

Mm-hmm. So the crown takes a disproportionate risk while we underwrite people who have a much longer runway to make their decisions to follow in behind us than they may have, um, aspired to the beginning. And the third, third, I think, is the fact that Wellington doesn't know best. Yeah. Wellington needs to work with the regions.

If you go in and dictate this is what you need to do, there's a high probability to fall over. Mm. As we've seen in all our, all aspects of life. Unless there's a real drive from the local people, the local community, this is what we want. This is part of our DNA, how we make things better, then you don't have that local commitment to support. It doesn't matter how much money you put it.

Yep. So much. Um, thank you for raising here. Um, because you know, as you all know, there are often issues when, when with Maori developments about multiple ownership, uh, raising capital. So what are the key things you've learned about enabling, um, Maori business development or supporting Iwi hapu development?

Yeah, So we've, we've developed various ways of supporting hapu and iwi businesses, including the Manama UA program, uh, sorry, UA Maori program where we have not taken security over the land, which is the biggest block, as you know, for raising money from banks.

And we've almost gone back to the model of how the stock and station agents, if anybody can remember, David will, how they used to operate in New Zealand, where they provided the working capital for farms and they recovered their, their working capital from part of the milk check, part of the receipts from the, from the fleece or from the, the grapes and the apples that were run.

And we've taken a similar approach to try and help fin Maori with long term capital that's weighted for repayment once the business is actually generating revenue again, uh, rather than looking for, uh, you know, snappy repayments and, and interest rates. Um, mm-hmm. And then I think on, on, in terms of, of fia, it's been, uh, you know, we'll stand alongside you until you've settled.

We'll stand alongside you until you're in a position so that that opportunity can be developed ahead of you actually having the cash to tip into the business yourself. Thank you. Alright. Um, members Just remind that this review briefing is, um, for 20 23, 20 24, um, annual we'll review. Yeah. Uh, supplementary Dr. Hamish.

Excellent. Thank you. Um, and you talked about, um, funding tech and emerging emerging industries. Where does, where does your remit start and where does it stop, uh, when it comes to maybe other players in the field, like, uh, New Zealand Capital Growth partners or, or, or something like that? How, how'd you work out where, where, where you, your organization should be playing versus somewhere else?

Someone else? Um, look, we, we make it, we, we, we, we never play in a space we're not invited into, and we will always default to existing partners, but we are quite often asked to, um, to support existing or potential technologies. For example, we are private equity, happy to play in the, uh, in the, uh, innovation IP space, but require infrastructure, we, we are probably better placed to support the infrastructure.

PE doesn't like playing in infrastructure and hard assets, so we, it, it, it's a mix of those, um, of those models. But, but we don't, we, we are very careful not to crowd out. Um, uh, I don't think we've seen any pushback from anyone that we've crowded anyone out in that space. Um, uh, because we are genuinely, we are generally seen as the player that people, because we don't push, we, these projects come to us.

If we are interested in a space that we might want to see stuff, then we canvas that space to come to us. So it's moderated in that respect. Can you give us an example of one of the projects you may have funded in this area? In the, in the Emerging tech? Uh, there's a Geo 40. Yeah. Yeah.

So Geo 40 is a, is a, an extraction of silica from, um, from geothermal wells, uh, innovative, uh, technology. And we took a quite a significant set, took 19 point, $19.9 million. Um, and, um, we enabled them to get to their product. They had, they had a test site and they've developed that to commercialization.

They're now, um, it's not without traves, it's a difficult rocky road as many of these are. Um, but they're now come out the other side raised external capital, and we will be, we've got first rights to get out, uh, at the end of it when we're not needed. And the idea for us is that we can then use that capital, recycle it for new innovations as, as disruption occurs.

We need to be able to respond to those. So, um, getting people up on their feet and allowing them to have a chance as our, as our piece. And we're not necessarily sit there to stay, monetize that to its maximum, but to get back in and help someone else. Mm-hmm. That's good. So I'll ask, uh, next primary.

So as you said, John, in your, um, uh, opening remarks that, um, economic time set up, we all agree with that, but with that also comes risk management. So can you tell us what have you done, like, uh, anything you've done specifically in this year, which is being reviewed to manage risk given tough economic circumstances, other than what you were doing before?

Well, we've, we've had a series of of risk reviews. We, we have a risk register that is, um, is at front of mind at every meeting. And we, we moderate that for emerging risks against our portfolio, existing portfolio. Um, so we, we present that at every board meeting and that risk register is reviewed by officials and then the board will sign it off to make sure it's ex mixed.

It's understanding or sense of the risks that are presented in the risk register accord with the boards, um, the, the economic challenges, seeing financial performance of firms move up that risk register. So that would mean that we are much more, we, we now have, uh, a much deeper dive into the non-performing assets.

Particularly the important thing though, that our members need to be aware of is that we have a very limited tools. We've got effectively no new money that we can support. We can't support, um, uh, good, good firms that we want to see, uh, want to see for the system benefit survive.

Um, so, uh, that's a, that's a bit of a problem. Our tools are about making sure that we are flexible around security repayments for those that are, those that are worth supporting. But there's also a fast fail element that we need to be conscious of, that actually not all of the businesses that we've supported should be continued to be sort supported.

They've had to go at what they thought they could do, haven't achieved it, and we would then, um, and be inclined to fast fail those and, and move, move on to something else. So does that mean that you're planning to add more performance measures, uh, in here risk management plan? I, I dunno whether there's any more.

Um, Dr. Powell, I think it's more about the, the types of risks that are under review continues to morph and develop as economic times, uh, change. Right. So I know we're talking about 2024, but the most easiest thing to understand is with the tariffs coming in from President Trump, what's the impact that has on businesses in New Zealand?

How does that impact our portfolio? So that's another risk that's emerged that we will now have to take into account when we look at our portfolio across different sectors. But then to see how the risk is being mitigated, uh, you have to have some kind of measure put in place. Right? So how are you, are you balancing that?

Um, so that the, at the end of the day, it comes down to the financial performance of the business in terms of its ability to meet its obligations to us. And, uh, those risks are constantly being assessed. And where there is pressure on that business, just as we see from the banks, we're looking to see whether there's some form of mitigation in terms of a moratorium on repayments, extending the payment terms, flattening out the, the repayment profile or whatever it might be to give good businesses the chance to continue to trade, uh, to work their way

through the economic cycle. So. Alright. Um, another question from, Um, I think you made an interesting point earlier talking about the challenge of the three year cycle and politicking that happens when we are talking about long run investments, investments for boosting or building resilience in the economy in many respects.

Um, I'm wondering if we are thinking about the challenge of the design from Wellington, can you give some examples of where that, that has caused entanglement or, or, or problems? I mean, I'm, I'm certainly thinking of something that's happened more recently, which is the inland port at Moscow.

Um, definitely a decision from Wellington that's caused a whole lot of rupture in the deep south. Um, but in this period, have you got any examples of where the decisions come from on high and, and caused some challenges that you've had to had to manage?

I, I think that, uh, thank you that, I think the biggest challenge we've seen is when there's a new fund that emerges in the criteria change. So when the provincial growth fund was established in 20 17, 20 18, that had a very, very broad remit. Uh, and there weren't many parts of, well, there were some sectors that were outta play and they, and they remained to the state, the three metropolitan areas, defense, health, housing, police, you know, justice, the typical things.

But the funds since then have had, have been more prescriptive in terms of either the sectors or the type of, of, of assets that the crown that the government wants to support. So we've had ones that focus specifically on queenstown, ones that focused on wood processing, ones that focused on cyclone recovery efforts and, and supporting agricultural businesses to get back on their feet.

Uh, this one is very much focused on infrastructure, uh, and hard infrastructure. And so it's, it's quite hard for local communities, local government and local businesses to suddenly go, oh, we've been working on this under the old scheme and been working through our plans and getting all the, all the work done to business case and feasibility starts to justify that.

And then we get to a change and the, the new fund has got different criteria. I think that's one of the more difficult things that we've observed. Mm-hmm. And so you get quite strong representations from local communities, government and business about, well, can't you make an exception?

Or, you know, this is a really, really good project. And that, that's, that becomes quite a challenging conversation. Alright. A sub entry. And, and I guess, um, those conversations perhaps are valuable because you're, you are also getting a sense of what the need is out there. Yep. Is there a, is there a, uh, any ability to, to modify, to adapt, to, to change the criteria?

I'm, what I'm particularly thinking of is the South Indian Future Program that has been underway for such a long time and the hard infrastructure questions are not the priority immediately. Mm. But for the success of that project, it will require hard infrastructure, but it needs that flexibility.

So is is there that, that, is there any ability to, to modify, to, to be more adaptable, to meet The need? Yeah, I think we're in a tough place at the moment to move outside the criteria, but having said that, ministers, the decision making ministers ultimately do have the ability to say, this is a very good project that the Crown should support and can take that to whatever it is, the authorizing group to make that, or to agree to that modification or that variation I should say.

Alright. Uh, new primary from Dr. Debon. Um, a little bit aligned to what you were going with Scott. Um, first of all, congratulations for operating in a public good area, which is really, really difficult when you're dealing with market failure. Fully understand that I also was very interested, um, as you know, when I was on the advisory panel, but the difference between grants debt and equity.

So it'd be really good to hear from you around some of the boundaries of those things and what you've sensed has been, uh, very successful between those three ways of, uh, of attaching those things. And also, um, no, I'll leave it to a supplementary, yeah. Okay. Can I start that because this has been my hobby horse.

I think it's important for members to understand that the genesis of the Provincial Growth Fund was originally all going to be grants. And, and David you'll recall this, and we thought that was a, that was not the smartest use of capital. So we fought hard with treasury to have the Schedule four company created so we could have at least a chance of getting this money back if it, if it was all grants, we would have a very short conversation today because all that money would be gone with no chance of covering it.

Um, so we think we've, we've taken a, a risk in going into this debt equity, uh, and grant position, but we fight hard to avoid providing grants because the, for, for me, as a board member and as chair, the virtue of having this capital recycle for the benefit of future generations is as important as enabling one project.

It's not enough for me that it's a system intervention, not a individual firm intervention. So I'm very firm on this and, and I'll, I'll hand to talk more specifically about it, but we have run a crusade against grants and, and, um, uh, unless, unless they're absolutely necessary and they are necessary, particularly for f Maori and, and places where there is really, really deep market failure and where the system doesn't support any, uh, any security of any sort, but by and large we won't do that.

Yeah. Supplementary. So, um, just in terms of loans on concessionary terms, can you give us some sense of what that might look like? Perhaps some example. Do you want a go first or No? Well, I'll start and then Yeah, I'll throw, because we're pushing The Boundaries here, Aren. Yeah, yeah, Absolutely. That's Big. So the, the concession, so I go back to my earlier point.

What we've tried to do is not give money away and just on the grants, we have seen examples where some projects have been funded with grants and, and because it was, pardon my firm, but free money Okay. That the thing built, but whoever the recipient was, was normally local government.

There's been no planning around opex and the r and m bill, which is ongoing, which we were never in the business, right from day one of funding opex, it's always been CapEx in some way, shape, or form. So, uh, and therefore, but then in, in structuring the loans and equity investments, it was about structuring in such a way that we weren't putting a burden on the borrower from day one in terms of repayments and financial obligations.

So the terms were concessionary, they were longer terms, there was interest deferral or, or subsidized interest rates or repayments were, were pushed out until the revenue flows could support them. That difference from a bank market position, which is you pay interest on day one at four commercial rates and you start repaying in 12 months time.

That difference is called the concession. And that concession has a financial value and that hits the balance sheet, sorry, hits the p and l on the day that the first draw down of a loan takes place. You want to amplify on that or Did Beautiful. Um, I guess it's, it's an unusual, uh, company to account for.

Um, I'm not quite pleased that the, our auditors in the past have said we are unique. I never thought I'd, um, go that far. So most of our loans are concessionary. We calculate that concession. Um, internally there is a process to come up with what a market rate could look like. That's quite a different, well, it's a difficult process because we, we don't have a market, so there is a lot of judgment in, in how we come up with it.

There is judgment throughout the book and that is why Audit New Zealand do the emphasis of matter. There is a lot of judgment, but on the whole, we follow accounting standards, we apply where we can get independent judgements on what we are using as far as discount rates, valuations, the likes, um, and we present the accounts, we present a lot of information in the accounts so people reading them can kind of see where those judgments are and, and what assumptions we have made. Mm-hmm.

Mm-hmm. And sorry, just to finalize Dr. Wilson, uh, just for the members, it's not a write off, it's not as though this is in default. It's, it's just the way the accounting rules force us to account for this. And, and over time those concessions can be well back and they come into the, to the revenue, uh, stream.

It's good members. Any other questions? Looks like there are no more questions, so that's very good. Thank you, John. Thank you Robert. Thank you, Joanne. Thank You. Pleasure. Thank You for your interest. Thank you. Thank you.
